title: My Mate Bill
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s his saddle on the tie-beam,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And themן¿½s his spurs up there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the wall-plate over yonderן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You ken see they ainן¿½t a pair.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the daddy of all the stockmen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As ever come mustering here
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was killed in the flaming mulga,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A-yarding a bald-faced steer.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They say as heן¿½s gone to heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And shook off all worldly cares
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I canן¿½t sight Bill in a halo
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Set up on three blinded hairs.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In heaven! what next I wonder,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For strike me pink and blue,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If I see whatever in thunder
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½ll find for Bill to do.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d never make one of them angels,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With faces as white as chalk,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All wool to the toes like hoggets,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wings like an eagle-hawk.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He couldnן¿½t ן¿½arp for apples,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His voice had tones as jarred,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And heן¿½d no more ear than a bald-faced steer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or calves in a branding yard.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He could sit on a bucking brumbie
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like a nob in an easy chair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And chop his name with a greenhide fall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the flank of a flying steer.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He could show them saints in glory
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The way that a fall should drop,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But sit on a throneן¿½not William,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unless they could make it prop.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He mightnן¿½t freeze to the seraphs,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or chum with the cherubim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But if ever them seraph johnnies
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Get a-poking it like at himן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well! if thereן¿½s hide in heaven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And silk for to make a lash,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½ll yard ן¿½em all in the Jasper Lake
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a blinded lightning flash.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If the heavenly hosts get boxed now,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As mobs most always will,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whoן¿½ll cut ן¿½em out like William,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or draft on a camp like Bill?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An ן¿½orseman would find it awkward
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At first with a push that flew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But blame my cats if I know what else
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½ll find for Bill to do.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s hard if there ainן¿½t no cattle,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And perhaps theyן¿½ll let him sleep,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wake him up at the judgment
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To draft those goats and sheep.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s playing it low on William,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But perhaps heן¿½ll buckle to,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To show them high-toned seraphs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What a Mulga man can do.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If they saddles a big-boned angel,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a turn of speed, of course,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As can spiel like a four-year brumbie,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And prop like an old camp horse,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And puts Bill up with a snaffle,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A four or five inch spur,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And eighteen foot of greenhide
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To chop the blinded furן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½ll yard them blamed Angoras
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a way that itן¿½s safe to swear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will make them tony seraphs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sit back on their thrones and stare.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
